items.title: The merits of undergraduate student samples
items.author: Jennifer M. Talarico
items.content: Undergraduate student samples in psychological research are commonly critiqued as ‘convenience samples’. However, they can also be ideally representative for certain research questions.

items.title: Artificial intelligence is not a substitute for human intelligence
items.content: As artificial intelligence (AI) permeates society and daily life, psychologists are investigating interactions between AI and human cognition, behaviour and values.

items.title: Memorability of visual stimuli and the role of processing efficiency
items.author: Lore Goetschalckx
items.content: Certain items are better remembered than others across individuals, a property known as memorability. In this Review, Bainbridge and colleagues detail memorability effects in perception, working memory and long-term memory, and consider the role of processing efficiency in memory.

items.title: How physical information is used to make sense of the psychological world
items.author: Minjae J. Kim
items.content: Reasoning about minds and reasoning about physical objects are governed by two distinct systems. In this Perspective, Liu et al. review research from developmental psychology and cognitive neuroscience that provides evidence for the interaction between psychological and physical reasoning systems.

items.title: Light as chronotherapy for depressed mood
items.author: Angus C. Burns
items.content: Light as chronotherapy for depressed mood

items.title: Individual differences in violent extremism
items.author: John F. Dovidio
items.content: In this Review, Obaidi et al. propose that understanding violent extremism requires integration of trait-descriptive models (such as Big Five and HEXACO) with process-oriented frameworks that outline mechanisms in social reactivity, needs and mindsets that make some individuals more at risk for engaging in violent extremism.

items.title: Dual-process theory and decision-making in large language models
items.author: David P. McGovern
items.content: The performance of large language models (LLMs) is often compared to human performance on decision-making tasks. In this Review, Brady and colleagues examine LLM outputs through the lens of dual-process theory, considering the hallmarks of System 1 and System 2 human decision-making.

items.title: Integrating cognitive neuroscience and clinical perspectives on metacognitive mechanisms in psychopathology
items.author: Tobias U. Hauser
items.content: Cognitive neuroscience and clinical psychology have made substantial advances in knowledge about metacognitive processes, but these fields have progressed in parallel. In this Review, Seow et al. integrate the literature to start a cross-field dialogue that will improve the understanding and treatment of psychopathology.
